coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent ‘turn-on’ porphyrin CQD nanoprobes for selective sensing of heavy metal ions
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Suppan, Thangamani
Bhattacharjee, Rama Ranjan
Pichumani, Moorthi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a one-step pyrolysis technique is employed to synthesize porphyrin based carbon quantum dots (Porp-CQDs). The as-synthesized Porp-CQDs permit fluorescence “on/off” strategies to detect heavy metal ions. The structural formation of the Porp-CQDs with optical properties is confirmed through characterization techniques like UV–visible, fluorescence spectroscopy, HR-TEM, AFM, X-ray diffraction, and FT-IR studies. Porp-CQDs have an average particle size of 2.4 nm and a spherical shape with a semi-crystalline appearance. The excitation wavelength appears at 434 nm corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of the soret band between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels with emission at 670 nm. The CV curves of the modified Porp-CQDs/GCE at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and Porp-CQDs/GCE exhibits good oxidation and reduction peaks at 1.43 V and − 0.36 V, respectively. The metal ion sensing mechanism is driven through the structure of the porphyrin core which makes it easy to form transition complexes with various metal ions in the central cage of the system. Porphyrins tagged on CQDs have been used as active sensing components in this work. The Porp-CQDs effectively sensed the Zn2+ metal ions with a limit detection of 75 μM in bore well water analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bore well water
Fluorescence
Heavy metal ions
Optical sensor
Porphyrin carbon quantum dots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors sincerely acknowledge The Management, SNR Sons Charitable Trust, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, India for providing an Institutional Postdoctoral research development fellowship. RRB thanks DST WTI project ( DST/TMD-EWO/WTI/2K19/EWFH/2019/217 ) for supporting the work. We also thank the PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for providing basic research facilities. Thanks to Dr. Goutam Pramanik of IUC Kolkata for light scattering experiments.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV spectra of Porp-CQDs as shown in (A) and TPPS4 as shown in (B).
FT-IR Spectra of TPPS4 and Porp-CQDs.
Photoluminescence spectra of TPPS4 and Porp-CQDs at excitation and emission spectra as shown in (A); Excitation-independent emission characteristics of Porp-CQDs nanostructure as shown in (B).

HR-TEM analysis of Porp-CQDs at various magnifications as shown in (A), the average size distribution of the histogram plot shown in (B), XRD analysis with identified planes in (C), and 2D and 3D surface morphology of the AFM investigation of Porp-CQDs in (D).
Photoluminescence Spectral Analysis of Porp-CQDs complex formation with.
Photoluminescence spectral analysis of Porp-CQDs complex formation with a) Zn2+ 0–3 μM; b) 0–5 μM; c) 0–100 μM; and d) 0–120 μM metal ion.

Photoluminescence spectral analysis of A) TPPS4 in the examination of bore well water at emission wavelengths ranging from 450 to 800 nm at 0 to 250 μM; B) Porp-CQDs complex formation of Zn2+ metal ion in bore well water analysis at 450–800 nm range; C) Porp-CQDs complex formation enhancement of Zn2+ metal ion in bore well water analysis at 450–550 nm range; D) Energy transfer diagram of Porp-CQDs complex formation with Zn2+ ion; E) UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation method for detecting metal ion interactions in the Porp-CQDs by fluorescence response and F) LOD of Porp-CQDs system on detection of Zn2+ metal ion in bore well water analysis.
Average size distribution of as-synthesized Porp-CQDs as shown in (a), addition of bore well water in Porp-CQDs as shown in (b), and Zn2+ ion detection in bore well water as shown in (c).
Shows the formation of the Porp-CQDs complex with various metal ions in the bore well water analysis in the visible (A) and ultraviolet regions (B) after 90 days of experiment.
A schematic representation of the synthesis of Porp-CQDs.
Absorption maxima and solubility of Porp-CQDs in different solvents.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent ‘turn-on’ porphyrin CQD nanoprobes for selective sensing of heavy metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Vattamalaipalayam, NGGO post, Coimbatore 641022, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotech Research Innovation and Incubation Centre (NRIIC), PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Avinashi Road, Coimbatore 641004, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanotech Research Innovation and Incubation Centre (NRIIC)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotech Research Innovation and Incubation Centre (NRIIC), PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, Avinashi Road, Coimbatore – 641004, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sister Nivedita University, New town, Kolkata 700156, West Bengal, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sister Nivedita University, New town, Kolkata 700156, West Bengal, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Nanoscience and Technology, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Vattamalaipalayam, NGGO post, Coimbatore 641022, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a one-step pyrolysis technique is employed to synthesize porphyrin based carbon quantum dots (Porp-CQDs). The as-synthesized Porp-CQDs permit fluorescence “on/off” strategies to detect heavy metal ions. The structural formation of the Porp-CQDs with optical properties is confirmed through characterization techniques like UV–visible, fluorescence spectroscopy, HR-TEM, AFM, X-ray diffraction, and FT-IR studies. Porp-CQDs have an average particle size of 2.4 nm and a spherical shape with a semi-crystalline appearance. The excitation wavelength appears at 434 nm corresponding to the π-π* electronic transition of the soret band between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels with emission at 670 nm. The CV curves of the modified Porp-CQDs/GCE at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and Porp-CQDs/GCE exhibits good oxidation and reduction peaks at 1.43 V and − 0.36 V, respectively. The metal ion sensing mechanism is driven through the structure of the porphyrin core which makes it easy to form transition complexes with various metal ions in the central cage of the system. Porphyrins tagged on CQDs have been used as active sensing components in this work. The Porp-CQDs effectively sensed the Zn2+ metal ions with a limit detection of 75 μM in bore well water analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The family of porphyrin-based structural sensing compounds has drawn attention to the detection of heavy metal ions in environmental pollutants [1]. Such a simple system of organization encourages a wide variety of activities and makes its application studies even more exciting. Due to the wide range of applications, various studies have focused on changing its shape and structure [2–6]. Porphyrin and its derivatives have significant electron-donating and accepting properties [7–9]. The finest fields of utilization of porphyrin and metallo porphyrins' are theoretical, industrial, analytical, and medical applications [10]. In the specialty field, porphyrins are active sensing components used as optical, fluorescent, electrochemical, amperometric, stochastic, colorimetric, potentiometric, voltammetric, impedimetric, and photoelectrochemical sensors [11]. Polymeric coordination with porphyrins is widely used in energy storage applications such as optical and electronic devices, solar energy and enzyme mimics [12]. In recent research, porphyrin-based nanomaterials have been used for ion sensing. These materials suffer from certain disadvantages like photostability and self-quenching issues. In order to improve such properties, porphyrin like molecules such as chlorophyll has been tagged or coordinated with nanoparticles like gold nanoparticle [5]. These conjugates show exciting features like enhanced photo stability and their ability of enhanced sensing. In this manuscript, we test the conjugate between carbon quantum dots with porphyrin.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) or carbon dots (CDs) have played an important role in a wide range of applications in recent years [13]. The unique properties of CQDs make them very robust and intelligent nanomaterial in the field of industrial and interdisciplinary applications [14]. The most interesting factor about carbon dot is their photoluminescence property (PL) and their size unique structural morphologies [15]. The PL mechanism depends on either excitation-dependent or excitation-independent emission properties [16]. CQDs tagged with small organic molecules with primary and secondary amine groups, inorganic, polymeric, and bioactive functionalization can be prepared by simple effective methods [17]. Sujata et al. stated that combining carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with porphyrins can be more important for achieving fine molecular nanostructures than their components [18–21]. Thangamani et al. have shown that the emission properties of DHPM are enhanced in conjugation with CQDs. The conjugates can be used for enhanced bio-imaging studies [22]. Several reports are available on the combination of porphyrins with carbon dots and their derivatives in ion sensing studies. Nevertheless, porphyrin molecule-based sensors are capable of establishing metal-ion interactions in an aqueous medium at the multi-ion detection level [23]. Zhen et al. (2020) in a recent publication reviewed porphyrin-based materials used for various metal ion detectors. They identified fifty different types of porphyrin-based materials for the detection of several heavy metal ions apart from Zn2+ ions in real water analysis [21]. Large amounts of zinc are deposited primarily from industrial wastewater treatment plants. Excess zinc contamination in borehole water can cause hair loss, diarrhea, nail disease, and eye problems.

The goal of this study is to tag porphyrin with carbon quantum dots (Porp-CQDs) to improve its optical properties. A one-step pyrolysis technique is employed to synthesize porphyrin-based carbon quantum dots and it permits fluorescence “on/off” strategies to detect heavy metal ions. The CQDs synthesized in this work possess an average particle size of 2.4 nm and a spherical shape with a semi-crystalline appearance. PL spectra exhibit excitation-independent emission characteristics associated with the π-π* electronic transition of the Soret band between the HOMO and LUMO energy levels with emission at 670 nm. Porp-CQDs is investigated in borehole water analysis and specifically identified Zn2+ metal ions at a LOD concentration of 75 μM. The uniqueness of the present manuscript focuses primarily on the visual color phenomenon of sensing heavy metal ions in the presence of UV light and their stability at room temperature.

Tetra phenyl sulfonated porphyrin (TPPS4) is synthesized by a conventional method, Citric acid monohydrate 99 % (Loba chemicals, India), and Double distilled water is used for all preparation. The different metal salts of Sodium chloride (NaCl), Potassium chloride (KCl), Nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2), Zinc acetate (Zn(CH₃CO₂)₂), Manganese (II) acetate (CH3CO2)2Mn), Copper (II) chloride (CuCl2) and Cobalt (II) chloride (CoCl2) are purchased from Loba chemicals, India.

Experiments with various metal ion solutions are first initiated to detect the presence of metal ions in borehole water. This process is easily observed with the naked eye. Metal ions such as cobalt, nickel, manganese, copper, and zinc are selected for study. Each ion solution is prepared at a concentration of 1 × 10
−3 mM. First, the Porp-CQDs (0.1 wt/vol%) is taken, then metal ions are added separately and their binding is confirmed by their color changes. Metal ion sensitivity assessment is performed by fluorescence spectra.

UV–Vis spectra recorded on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer scanned in the range of 200–800 nm. FT-IR measurements are performed using Shimadzu in the range of 4500 to 500 cm−1 and samples are analyzed with KBr adduct. To characterize the size of the quantum dots, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) studies are performed using a JEOL-2011. XRD analyses are using powder samples equipped with X-ray diffractometer (RICH SEIFERT-XRD 3000P with X-ray generator - Cu, 30 mA, 45 kV, and wavelength 1.54 Å). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) data is collected on an Agilent spectrofluorometer (Carry Eclipse). An atomic force microscope (AFM) is scanned with a T-MDT NTEGRA Aura system. Cyclic voltammetry studies are performed on CH instruments (CHI600E, USA).

1.5 g of citric acid and 0.1 g of TPPS4 are dissolved in 20 ml of distilled water with continuous stirring for 1 h. After that, the TPPS4 solution is gradually mixed with citric acid and continuously stirred for 1 h. The TPPS4 solution is probably dark purple at first but turns green when citric acid is added. After continuous stirring for 1 h, the green solution is placed in a water bath at 70 °C for complete evaporation of water. To carbonize the porphyrin and citric acid are placed in the alumina boat and heated at 190 °C for 4 h. Finally, the dark green powder is tested and identified as Porp-CQDs by characterization techniques. A schematic representation of the synthesis of Porp-CQDs is given in Scheme 1 .

In the TPPS4 system, M represents the vacant site where the metal ion (M) is bound to TPPS4, but no specific metal ion is present during the reaction given in the schematic illustration. Several reports indicate a similar structure to represent TPPS4 as well and metal ion interaction with TPPS4 occurs depending on their size, charge, and spin amplification, so that metal ions fit into the central pore of the porphyrin ring, forming typical metalloporphyrins [10]. The metal complex of the porphyrin method combines the porphyrin with a metal salt (i.e. K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn3+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, and Pb2+, etc..) to form a metal-containing porphyrin complex.

We have recently demonstrated that conjugation of CQD to organic macromolecules like dihydropyrimidinones (DHPM) improves the emission properties of the CQD-DHPM conjugate and that the same can be effective in cell imaging studies [22]. The enhanced emission characteristics are attributed to extended conjugation and charge transfer from the CQDs to the molecule surface. In the present work, Porphyrin plays an important role in acting as a stabilizer for CQDs by attaching on to the CQDs surface. In the absence of porphyrin, CQDs are not observed to be formed. Here, CQDs are prepared in situ, i.e. the precursor of CQDs (citric acid here) is mixed with porphyrins, and then the material is pyrolyzed. During the pyrolysis process, CQDs are formed and stabilized by porphyrin present in the reaction medium. The stabilization of Porp-CQDs and its metal ion sensing characterization methods are described using the following techniques.

In the case of porphyrins, the visible absorption spectra are strongly modified when two of the internal nitrogen atoms are protonated or when metal atoms are added or removed from the macrocycle [24]. The addition of metal ions changes the color of the solution, which is readily visible to the naked eye and produces distinctive fluorescent colors. Such color changes improve the understanding of the Porp-CQDs charge transfer spectra and the d-d transition in spectroscopic methods [25]. The d orbitals are a common characteristic of transition elements. These orbitals are degenerate, indicating that they all have the same level of energy when the metal ions happen to be unbounded. Due to their unique symmetries and the inductive effects of the ligands on the electrons, the d orbitals split and enter the degenerate phase (they have different energy levels). Different ions and atom types have different energies, which produce different colors [26–28]. Bearing positive or negative charges on porphyrins can cause detectable spectral changes when combined with metals [29]. In contrast to HOMOs, which are determined as a1u and a2u orbitals LUMOs are found to be a degenerate pair of eg orbitals and these orbital changes result in two excited states. Q-bands and Soret bands have higher energy levels, while orbital mixing splits the two levels into different energy levels [30–38]. Accordingly, the electronic absorption spectra of a typical porphyrin consists of two distinct bands. The Soret or B band is associated with the initial transition, which takes place between the ground state and the second excited state (S
0
-S
2 ). The absorbance varies from 380 to 500 nm depending on whether the porphyrin is meso-substituted. Around 500 nm and 750 nm, a weak transition to the first excited state (S
0
- S
1 ) takes place, known as the Q bands. Porphyrins can be conveniently and precisely used to monitor guest-binding functions due to their excellent spectroscopic features through the incorporation of 18π - electrons [39–41].

The formation of Porp-CQDs and the existence of porphyrin in the conjugate is confirmed using UV–vis spectroscopy. The presence of porphyrin is confirmed by the observation of a very intense Soret band and four weak Q-bands. UV absorption spectra shows strong intensity peaks at 434 nm (0,0) for S
0 → 0 S
2 transition and Q bands at 647 nm (0,1) for S
0 → S
1 transitions (Fig. 1A). A sample of pure TPPS4 shows Soret band at 412 nm in excitation and Q-band values appear at 515 nm for the Q (0,1) state as shown in Fig. 1B [39].

The absorption maximum depends on the dielectric constant of the surrounding sample. The absorption spectra of Porp-CQDs in various solvents are shown in the supplementary information (SI) Fig. S1 and summarized in Table 1 below.

The formation of Porp-CQDs is further characterized by FT-IR. In the FT-IR spectra, the free base TPPS4 (black line) in Fig. 2 exhibits a moderate OH stretching vibration at 3468 cm−1. Fig. 2 shows the Porp-CQDs (red line) spectra, which exhibit a significant peak with a peak shift at 3483 cm−1. For Porp-CQDs, there is a small peak at 2966 cm−1 (CH), but not for TPPS4. Modifications of the -SH group may involve the formation of covalent bonds with a carbon source [39]. Due to the CC stretching of the phenyl ring, metal chelates of TPP exhibit very intense absorption bands at 1647 cm−1. At low frequencies, the pyrrole ring CH rocking vibrations form two distinct bands at 969 and 866 cm−1, respectively, which are not evident in the Porp-CQDs spectra. FTIR spectral data in Fig. 2 shows that the sharp and broad peaks decreased after CQDs conjugate formation.

CQDs exhibit tuneable photoluminescence. One of the most acceptable luminescence mechanisms is the involvement of surface trap states [42]. Along with surface states, the presence of surface functional groups identified as molecular states between CO and CN can influence the fluorescence of CQDs. The in situ synthesis process used in this study can provide sufficient interface between the CQDs produced with porphyrin surface and that in turn may enhance the fluorescence intensity of porphyrins. A carbon dot possesses excitation-dependent emission properties between 400 nm and 600 nm and porphyrin possess emission between 600 nm and 750 nm [43]. Yadola G. et al. reported that CQDs shows fluorescence emissions between 400 nm and 600 nm and a molecular surface state emission between 600 nm and 750 nm [44]. Porp-CQDs and TPPS4 solutions are subjected to fluorescence studies at different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 3 A&B. Fig. 3A shows the excitation and emission spectra of pure TPPS4 and Porp-CQDs at 350 nm to 800 nm excitation wavelengths. Fig. 3B shows the emission spectra of Porp-CQDs. The excitation wavelength is varied between 370 nm to 450 nm. The emission maximum shows a constant value of 670 nm and excitation-independent emission properties. Porp-CQDs exhibits excitation wavelengths of 431 nm (Q 0,1) and 671 nm (Q 0,0) in PL spectra (Fig. 3A). TPPS4 exhibits an excitation wavelength of 410 nm (Q 0,0) and an emission wavelength of two peaks at 643 nm (Q 0,0) and 712 nm (Q 0,1) attributed to the excitation-independent emission properties of TPPS4 (supplementary information (SI) Fig. S2). The comparative spectral features observed in UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Figs. 2 & 3) may indicate that the porphyrin binds effectively to the CQDs [45].

HR-TEM analysis is used to characterize the structural features of Porp-CQDs and to confirm the formation of CQDs. HR-TEM data helps to evaluate particle size, shape, dispersion, and crystallinity. Fig. 4A&B indicates the formation of Porp-CQDs with semi-crystalline and spherical particles. The in-plane lattice spacing is focused at 0.24 nm. The average particle size of the formed CQDs is 2.4 nm and it is measured using ImageJ software. A structural feature like d-spacing and the EDS spectra is also recorded. According to existing research results, CQDs are present as evident from lattice fringes and d-spacing plane obtained from TEM examinations [46,47]. The EDS measurement is carried out on the samples using the TEM equipment. The EDS data revealed that only carbon atoms are present in the spectra (in the supplementary information (SI) Fig. S3). Sarah Regan et al., in 2022 studied porphyrin-based graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) in a carbon-based mesh, where small particles are reduced to the background and it is very difficult to accurately determine the size of the lattice [46].

XRD and AFM data is used to further substantiate the Porp-CQDs nanostructures. XRD data shows peaks at 21.87°, (002 planes; d = 0.41 nm), 2θ 42.13° (111 planes; d = 0.21 nm), 2θ 49.14° (011 planes; d = 0.19 nm), and 72.67° (220 planes; d = 0.12 nm) confirming the assembly of Porp-CQDs with semi-crystalline nature in Fig. 4C. AFM 2D and 3D topography analysis shows the presence of Porp-CQDs in Fig. 4D with an average size of 7.82 nm [48].

The redox characteristic of Porp-CQDs is studied by cyclic voltammetry analysis. In the experiments, Porp-CQDs coated on glassy carbon are used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. The modified Porp-CQDs/GCE is immersed in 1 M NaOH electrolyte and has a voltage range of −1.5 V to 1.8 V. Fig. 5 shows the CV curves of the modified Porp-CQDs/GCE at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Porp-CQDs/GCE exhibits good oxidation and reduction peaks at 1.43 V and − 0.36 V, respectively.

CQDs and porphyrin-based CQDs have been widely used in the field of sensing metal ions by fluorescence analysis due to their tuneable fluorescence, high stability, and uniform dispersion [49,50]. CQDs easily interact between metal ions and lead to enhancement or quenching of fluorescence. Photoluminescence spectroscopy is used to study changes due to valence electron rearrangements in porphyrins [51,52]. The color of transition metal complex solution changes with different metals, metal oxidation state, and metal d-electrons [53-55]. The following section presents the fluorescence spectroscopic sensitivity studies on the electrostatic interactions between Co2+ and Zn2+ metal ions and Porp-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra of Porp-CQDs show an emission peak at 670 nm under a 430 nm excitation (Fig. 6 ). However, differences in color and spectra are observed in the Porp-CQDs system with the addition of 10–20 μM concentrations of Co2+ metal ion. At higher concentrations of Co2+, 30–120 μM, two different emission peaks at 670 nm and 728 nm are observed. Co2+ metal ions can be positively identified using fluorescence spectra and visible color changes in solution. The strong bonding interaction between Co2+ ions and porphyrin (M represents the vacant site where the metal ion (M) is bound to TPPS4) on the surface of CQDs facilitating the easy binding of metal ions. Through electrostatic interactions, Porp-CQDs begin forming metal ion complexes as the concentration of Co2+ increases. Electrons in the conduction band and defect-induced states of Porp-CQDs are readily transferred to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Co2+ (Fig. 6). The change in the spectra may be due to the outer electronic structure of Co2+ which is 3d 74S 2 and d orbitals are half-filled.
Co2+ metal ion.

Zn2+ ions are readily adsorbed onto the negatively charged Porp-CQDs surface (M represents the vacant site where the metal ion (M) is bound to TPPS4) due to electrostatic attractions. As small amounts of Zn2+ metal ion (0–3 μM) are introduced into the Porp-CQDs solution, there are immediate changes in the color and absorption spectra, as illustrated in Fig. 7 (inserted in). The same solution shows the visible emission spectra at 607 nm and 674 nm. As the amount of Zn2+ metal ions is increased (30–120 μM; Fig. 7 a to d), the color or spectra remained unchanged. At the same time, two distinct emission bands at 607 and 659 nm are observed (Fig. 7). There is a significant fluorescence quenching of Porp-CQDs under the addition of Zn2+ and a unique coordination interaction between Zn2+ ions and the Porp-CQDs conjugate. The above observations indicate that Porp-CQDs play an extensive role in specific coordination interactions of Co2+ and Zn2+ for colorimetric sensing of these heavy metal ions.

The addition of various metal ions such as Cu2+, Ni2+, and Mn2+ does not produce similar spectra as observed in the previous sections. The above method additionally throws evidence from the UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation method for detecting metal ion interactions in the Porp-CQDs fluorescence response (Fig. 8E). Due to their complex formation properties with metal ions, the Porp-CQDs (Green color), Porp-CQDs/ bore well water (Blue color), and Porp-CQDs/Zn2+ (Blue color) metal ion solutions generate significantly varied fluorescence hue as responses. Except for the Zn2+ metal ion, the same concentrations of other metal ions do not show any fluorescence reactions as shown in Fig. 8C. As a result, the LOD of 75 μM thorough experiments are confirmed by the Porp-CQDs system, which detects Zn2+ in bore well water as shown in Fig. 8F.

When the typical metal ions connect with a high concentration of sensing material it leads to an inner filter effect. Through this effect, it is not possible to know whether this sensing is due to the nature of the material or the concentration of metal ions [38]. The Porp-CQDs and Porp-CQDs/bore well water (BW) samples in Figs. 9a and b are measured by the particle size measurement (DLS). The size of Pop-CQDs as-synthesized is 2.2 nm, while the addition of BW shows a size of 3.3 nm, as evidenced by the DLS measurement. In Fig. 9c, the concentration has been slowly increased to 1000 μM, and the expected detection outcome is an increase in the Zn ion peak at 500 nm. Even at high concentrations, the Porp-CQDs can detect metal ions without forming any agglomeration or inner filter effect.

In the present study, UV-spectral investigation of water-soluble Porp-CQDs is used to confirm the coordination of complex formation with various heavy metal ions (Fig. S4). However, as various heavy metal ions are introduced to Porp-CQDs, the spectral band values change significantly. With every 1 × 10−3 mM addition of metal ions, the B-band values change with each metal ion listed in the table, with green to purple and orange colors appearing immediately after the addition of water samples (insert Fig. S4). The NMR data of TPPS4 is given in the supplementary information (SI) Fig. S5. Fig. 10 shows the formation of the Porp-CQDs complex with various metal ions in bore well water analysis in visible (A) and ultraviolet regions (B) after 90 days of the experiment and it is still stable.

In conclusion, a pyrolysis technique is used to synthesize porphyrin carbon quantum dots (Porp-CQDs), which are expected to directly sense specific heavy metal ions polluting our environment. The synthesized Porp-CQDs exhibit a spherical shape, semi-crystallinity, as well as an average size of 2.4 nm with excitation-independent emission and electrochemical characteristic properties. The CV curves of the modified Porp-CQDs/GCE at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 and Porp-CQDs/GCE exhibit good oxidation and reduction peaks at 1.43 V and − 0.36 V, respectively. Porp-CQDs are investigated in bore well water analysis and specifically identified Zn2+ metal ions at a LOD concentration of 75 μM. This study may be used to throw more light on the designing of optical sensors for heavy metal ion detection in future studies.

Thangamani Suppan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization. Rama Ranjan Bhattacharjee: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Moorthi Pichumani: Validation, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely acknowledge The Management, SNR Sons Charitable Trust, Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, India for providing an Institutional Postdoctoral research development fellowship. RRB thanks DST WTI project (DST/TMD-EWO/WTI/2K19/EWFH/2019/217) for supporting the work. We also thank the PSG Institute of Advanced Studies for providing basic research facilities. Thanks to Dr. Goutam Pramanik of IUC Kolkata for light scattering experiments.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111265.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ecofriendly remediation technologies for wastewater contaminated with heavy metals with special focus on using water hyacinth and black tea wastes: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Elbasiouny, H., Darwesh, M., Elbeltagy, H., Abo-alhamd Fatma G., Amer, A. A., Elsegaiy, M. A., Brevik, E. C, 2021. Ecofriendly remediation technologies for wastewater contaminated with heavy metals with special focus on using water hyacinth and black tea wastes: a review. Environmental Monitoring and Assessment, 193(7). doi:10.1007/s10661-021-09236-2

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. S. Thangamani currently working as an Institute Postdoctoral Fellow at Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore. She received her Ph.D. in Chemistry from PSG Institute of Advanced Studies, affiliated with Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, in 2022. She received her postgraduate and undergraduate degrees in Chemistry from PSGR Krishnammal College for Women, Coimbatore, affiliated with Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, from 2011 to 2015. She has 6 years of research experience and her research interests are in carbon-based nanomaterials for energy and health care applications. She published six technical research papers in peer-reviewed international journals and 4 research papers under progress. In 2020, she published 1 US-India CSIR Patent.

Dr. RamaRanjan Bhattacharjee is a chemistry professor at Sister Nivedita University in Kolkata's New Town. His areas of expertise include surface modification, nanoparticle synthesis, nanomaterial synthesis, material characterization, nanofabrication, and advanced materials. He previously worked as an Associate professor at PSG Institute of Advanced Studies in Coimbatore, India, and Amity University in Noida, India.

Dr. Moorthi Pichumani is currently working as Professor and Head of Department in the Nanoscience and Technology Department at Sri Ramakrishna Engineering College, Coimbatore, Tamilnadu. Formerly, he was the DST INSPIRE Faculty at the Department (B2014). He completed his Bachelor's Degree (with distinction and University rank) in Physics from Periyar University (affiliated to), Salem, in the year 2006. He completed his Masters' degree (with distinction and two gold medals) in Materials Science from PSG College of Technology, Coimbatore (Anna University, Chennai) in the year 2008. He completed his Ph.D. from the University of Navarra (Doctoral fellowship by the Asociación de Amigos), Spain in the year 2012. He has been awarded the Latin honor “Cum Laude” for the successful completion of doctoral studies. He has worked in various international projects from the Government of Navarra (Spain), Government of Spain, and the Government of Canada as a researcher. Research interest includes soft matter, nanomaterials, materials science, and nanofluids.
